learning_outcomes.description: Critically assess gaps in macroeconomic literature.
Design research questions and research projects to fill the gaps.
Apply empirical and/or quantitative tools to advance research projects.
Effective communication of research findings in academic presentation.

assessments.task_description:
Students apply the tools covered in lectures in a two-part exercise. Part A (common to all students): calibrate a version of the heterogeneous-agent model from the provided starter code, source and document real data to construct the calibration targets, and compute a macroeconomic policy counterfactual (for example, a tax or transfer reform) with distributional analysis. Part B (thesis-linked): extend the model toward the student’s own thesis question, or confront the model with data from the student’s own research, whether structural or empirical in approach. The submission includes the slides for the first presentation. Results are presented and defended in class in Week 8 (Presentation 1).

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission via Blackboard by the due date and time. In addition, students should email their codes for the computational part to the lecturer.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: Students deliver three presentations during the semester, in class. The first (Week 8) presents the results of the Empirical Assignment in academic seminar format, using the slides submitted with the assignment; no new analysis is expected. The second (Week 9) is a critical discussion of a published paper related to the student’s thesis field, also in academic format. The third (Week 11) is a dry run of the final presentation, addressed to a non-academic audience: part of the course’s training in communicating the same research to different audiences. Any presentation materials are submitted by 2pm on the day of the class. Students are expected to speak to their slides rather than read from a script or notes; delivery is part of the assessment criteria for each presentation. Each presentation is followed by questions and feedback, in which students are expected to demonstrate comprehension of the work they present.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Presentations are delivered in class. Presentation materials are submitted by 2pm on the day of the class.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: Students submit the abstract, the introduction, and the conclusion sections of their own thesis, applying the writing techniques discussed in lectures, together with a brief exploratory outlook (approximately one page) proposing a potential extension or application of their thesis topic using the quantitative tools developed in the course: what tool could be used, what question it could answer, and why that question is relevant. The outlook is a research pitch, not an implementation; no modelling or estimation work is expected. This is the same material students present and defend at the dry run and the final presentation session.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission via Blackboard by the due date and time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Each student presents their thesis research at the final presentation session, to an audience that may include invited guests from academia, industry and the public sector. Presentations are approximately 10 minutes, followed by questions, and should be accessible to a general audience without specialist knowledge of the topic. Further details are advised in class.

The presentation should include and demonstrate:

  • Clear articulation of the research question, methodology, and key findings using language accessible to non-specialists
  • Demonstration of critical understanding of the research context and significance
  • Application of presentation techniques taught in the workshop sessions

 

Evaluation

Students will be evaluated on factual accuracy, clarity of communication, organisation and structure, time management, effective use of visual aids, delivery without reading from a script, ability to effectively communicate their research to the audience, and more generally the demonstration of presentation skills learned throughout the semester.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Why models? Structural and empirical approaches to macroeconomic research

Why we need structural models: model-based counterfactuals and econometric estimates (VARs, local projections) might answer different research and policy questions, and knowing which tool fits which question is the first research skill. We examine the limits of representative-agent macroeconomics, the growing role of micro data in macro, and how the field evolved toward heterogeneous-agent modelling, closing with a live demonstration of the computational toolkit developed across the course. A curated self-study module on local projections, SVARs and factor models is posted this week as a supported pathway for empirically inclined students.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Why models? Structural and empirical approaches to macroeconomic research

We examine the limits of representative-agent macroeconomics, the growing role of micro data in macro, and how the field evolved toward heterogeneous-agent modelling, closing with a live demonstration of the computational toolkit developed across the course. A curated self-study module on local projections, SVARs and factor models is posted this week as a supported pathway for empirically inclined students.

learning_activities.topic:
The workhorse: from benchmark model to calibrated research tool

An overview of the heterogeneous-agent workhorse, followed by its use as a research tool with the complete starter code provided: extending the benchmark with a policy block (for example, taxes and transfers) and calibrating it to Australian wealth and income moments.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: The workhorse: from benchmark model to calibrated research tool
learning_activities.topic_description: The workhorse: from benchmark model to calibrated research tool An overview of the heterogeneous-agent workhorse, followed by its use as a research tool with the complete starter code provided: extending the benchmark with a policy block (for example, taxes and transfers) and calibrating it to Australian wealth and income moments.

learning_activities.topic:
Counterfactuals and transition dynamics

Computing macroeconomic policy counterfactuals in the calibrated model, with a tax reform as the running example. We compare steady states across policy regimes, then use the course code to compute and interpret the transition path between them: who gains and who loses along the way, and why long-run comparisons alone can mislead. These are the core exercises through which a structural model answers a research or policy question.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Counterfactuals and transition dynamics Computing macroeconomic policy counterfactuals in the calibrated model, with a tax reform as the running example. We compare steady states across policy regimes, then use the course code to compute and interpret the transition path between them: who gains and who loses along the way, and why long-run comparisons alone can mislead. These are the core exercises through which a structural model answers a research or policy question.

learning_activities.topic:
Distributional and welfare analysis; model selection in applications

Distributional and welfare analysis of macroeconomic policy change: Lorenz curves, Gini coefficients, top wealth and income shares, and identifying winners and losers across the distribution. Model selection, meaning the choice of question-driven extensions of the workhorse model, is demonstrated through applications across macroeconomic policy domains (monetary policy and fiscal policy), including how model counterfactuals and reduced-form evidence complement one another. The Empirical Assignment brief is released in this session; its common Part A builds directly on the hands-on exercises of Weeks 2 to 4.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Distributional and welfare analysis; model selection in applications

The Empirical Assignment brief is released in this session; its common Part A builds directly on the hands-on exercises of Weeks 2 to 4.

learning_activities.topic:
Research Communication Skills

Research communication session delivered with the UQ Academic Skills team, jointly with ECON6201, covering presentation skills (structuring an academic talk, designing effective slides, and what to highlight when presenting the same research to an academic audience versus non-academic audiences) and academic writing skills for the thesis.

Learning outcomes: L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Research Communication Skills Research communication session delivered with the UQ Academic Skills team, jointly with ECON6201, covering presentation skills (structuring an academic talk, designing effective slides, and what to highlight when presenting the same research to an academic audience versus non-academic audiences) and academic writing skills for the thesis.

learning_activities.topic:
Thesis application session

Each student maps the course toolkit to their own thesis, whether structural or empirical in approach, following a published dual track: structural students specify a model variant and experiment, while empirical students choose one of three model-as-lens pathways. Students produce a one-page research-design map and workshop it with structured peer feedback.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Thesis application session Each student maps the course toolkit to their own thesis, whether structural or empirical in approach, following a published dual track: structural students specify a model variant and experiment, while empirical students choose one of three model-as-lens pathways. Students produce a one-page research-design map and workshop it with structured peer feedback.

learning_activities.topic:
Writing in Macroeconomics

Review of foundational papers in macroeconomics as models of effective academic writing: structure, framing a contribution, motivating a research question from a gap in the literature, and the craft of abstracts, introductions and conclusions.

Learning outcomes: L01, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Writing in Macroeconomics Review of foundational papers in macroeconomics as models of effective academic writing: structure, framing a contribution, motivating a research question from a gap in the literature, and the craft of abstracts, introductions and conclusions.

learning_activities.topic:
Students' Presentations I

Each student delivers the first assessed presentation, communicating the results of their Empirical Assignment in academic seminar format: motivating the question from the literature, defending methodological choices, and presenting results with appropriate technical depth. The presentation draws directly on the submitted assignment materials; no new analysis is expected. Structured feedback from peers and the coordinator follows each talk.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Students' Presentations I Each student delivers the first assessed presentation, communicating the results of their Empirical Assignment in academic seminar format: motivating the question from the literature, defending methodological choices, and presenting results with appropriate technical depth. The presentation draws directly on the submitted assignment materials; no new analysis is expected. Structured feedback from peers and the coordinator follows each talk.

learning_activities.topic:
Students' Presentations II

Each student delivers the second assessed presentation: a critical discussion of a published paper related to their thesis field, positioning it within the literature and assessing its contribution. Structured feedback from peers and the coordinator follows each talk.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Students' Presentations II Each student delivers the second assessed presentation: a critical discussion of a published paper related to their thesis field, positioning it within the literature and assessing its contribution. Structured feedback from peers and the coordinator follows each talk.

learning_activities.topic:
No class: King’s Birthday public holiday

No class this week (King’s Birthday public holiday). The Writing Assignment is submitted online by Friday of this week, 5pm. An optional online consultation is offered during the week for questions on the Writing Assignment and the upcoming dry run.
learning_activities.topic_description: No class: King’s Birthday public holiday No class this week (King’s Birthday public holiday). The Writing Assignment is submitted online by Friday of this week, 5pm. An optional online consultation is offered during the week for questions on the Writing Assignment and the upcoming dry run.

learning_activities.topic:
Students' Presentations III

Each student delivers the third assessed presentation: a full dry run of their final research presentation, pitched to a non-academic audience, highlighting the research question, its real-world relevance and the implications of the findings, whether structural or empirical in approach. Detailed feedback is provided for incorporation before the final presentation.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Students' Presentations III Each student delivers the third assessed presentation: a full dry run of their final research presentation, pitched to a non-academic audience, highlighting the research question, its real-world relevance and the implications of the findings, whether structural or empirical in approach. Detailed feedback is provided for incorporation before the final presentation.

learning_activities.topic:
Students' Presentation Event

Each student delivers their final research presentation to an audience that may include invited guests from industry, academia and the public sector. Further details are advised in class.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Students' Presentation Event Each student delivers their final research presentation to an audience that may include invited guests from industry, academia and the public sector. Further details are advised in class.

learning_activities.topic:
Review and wrap-up

Review of the course: consolidating the heterogeneous-agent toolkit and the research communication skills built across the semester, and drawing together feedback from the final presentations for the last stage of the thesis.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
